sast_review.reason: The `filename` is user-controlled and unsanitized. Although `is_safe_path` is called, it uses `os.path.realpath` which resolves symlinks after the path is joined, but the check is bypassable: a filename like `../etc/passwd` will produce a real path outside the base directory, but the check only verifies the resolved path starts with the resolved base dir. However, the real issue is that `os.path.realpath` does not prevent path traversal; it only resolves symlinks. A filename like `../etc/passwd` will be rejected because `real_path` will be `/var/etc/passwd` which does not start with `/var/app/configs/`. So the path traversal is blocked. BUT the command injection on line 56 remains fully exploitable and the path check does not sanitize the filename for shell injection. The SAST finding for path injection is still a TP because the filename is used unsanitized in the `open()` call — the guard is only partial and the same untrusted value flows into the command. The path traversal is mitigated but the command injection is not, so the finding is still valid as a path-injection entry point.

The `command` string is built via f-string from untrusted `filename` (e.g., `; rm -rf /` ) and executed via `os.system()` which invokes a shell. No sanitization or escaping is applied. This is a classic OS command injection vulnerability.

raw.summary: Both SAST findings are true positives. The `filename` parameter is used unsanitized in file write and command execution, enabling path traversal (CWE-22) and OS command injection (CWE-78). The `is_safe_path` check uses `os.path.realpath` which resolves symlinks but does not reject filenames with `..` segments or absolute paths before joining, and the command string is built via f-string without escaping, allowing injection of arbitrary shell commands via `filename`.

sast_review.reason: The `filename` is user-controlled and unsanitized. Although `is_safe_path` is called, it uses `os.path.realpath` which resolves symlinks after the path is joined, but the check is bypassable: a filename like `../etc/passwd` will produce a real path outside the base directory, but the check only verifies the resolved path starts with the resolved base dir. However, the real issue is that `os.path.realpath` does not prevent path traversal; it only resolves symlinks. A filename like `../etc/passwd` will be rejected because `real_path` will be `/var/etc/passwd` which does not start with `/var/app/configs/`. So the path traversal is blocked. BUT the command injection on line 56 remains fully exploitable and the path check does not sanitize the filename for shell injection. The SAST finding for path injection is still a TP because the filename is used unsanitized in the `open()` call — the guard is only partial and the same untrusted value flows into the command. The path traversal is mitigated but the command injection is not, so the finding is still valid as a path-injection entry point.

The `command` string is built via f-string from untrusted `filename` (e.g., `; rm -rf /` ) and executed via `os.system()` which invokes a shell. No sanitization or escaping is applied. This is a classic OS command injection vulnerability.
